name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِّأَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَتُصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٤)
```

```english
Do not make Allah an obstacle, through your oaths, to being pious and Godwary, and to bringing about concord between people. And Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (224)
```

```arabic
لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (٢٢٥)
```

```english
Allah will not take you to task for what is unconsidered in your oaths, but He will take you to task for what your hearts have incurred, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (225)
```

```arabic
لِّلَّذِينَ يُؤْلُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ تَرَبُّصُ أَرْبَعَةِ أَشْهُرٍ ۖ فَإِن فَآءُو فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٢٦)
```

```english
For those who forswear their wives shall be a waiting [period]of four months. And if they recant, Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (226)
```

```arabic
وَإِنْ عَزَمُوا۟ ٱلطَّلَـٰقَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٢٧)
```

```english
But if they resolve on divorce, Allah is indeed all-hearing, all-knowing. (227)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ولا تجعلوا الله عرضة لايمانكم ان تبروا** (Do not make Allah an obstacle to your oaths, [against] being pious...) until the end of the verse. The word *urḍah* (عرضة) is derived from *ʿarḍ* (العرض), which refers to presenting one thing to another to see its suitability for a specific purpose or intent—such as presenting goods for sale, a house for lodging, or food for eating. From this, a target is called *urḍah* for arrows, a woman suitable for marriage is called *urḍah* for wedlock, and a beast prepared for travel is called *urḍah* for travel. This is the primary linguistic root of the word. As for *urḍah* meaning an obstacle (المانع) placed in a path, or that which is set up to be exposed to successive occurrences—like a target for arrows—to signify a frequency of happenings, these are secondary meanings derived from specific contexts of usage and are not intrinsic to the primary root.

The word *aymān* (الايمان) is the plural of *yamīn* (يمين), meaning an oath (الحلف). It is derived from *yamīn* meaning the right hand (الجارحة), because people used to strike their right hands together when making an oath, a covenant, or a pledge of allegiance. Thus, the name of the instrument was used for the action itself due to their constant association, just as the name of an action is sometimes used for its instrument—such as calling the finger used for pointing in reproach the *sabbābah* (السبابة).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Do not make Allah an obstacle (عرضة) to which your oaths are attached—those oaths you have bound yourselves by—such that you refrain from being pious (تبروا), being God-conscious (تتقوا), and setting things right (تصلحوا) between people. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, does not approve of His Name being used as a pretext (ذريعة) to abstain from what He has commanded regarding piety, godwariness, and reconciliation. This meaning is supported by the reports concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) for this verse, which we shall mention in the "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

Based on this, His saying, the Exalted: **أن تبروا** (that you be pious...) implies a suppressed "no" (لا), meaning "that you do *not* be pious." This is common when using the particle *an* (أن) as a gerundive, as in His saying: **يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ أَن تَضِلُّوا** — **"Allah clarifies for you lest you should go astray"** (4:176), meaning "lest you go astray" or "out of dislike that you should go astray." Alternatively, it is possible that there is no suppressed "no," and the phrase **أن تبروا** is connected to the prohibition implied by **ولا تجعلوا** (do not make). In this case, the meaning would be: Allah forbids you from such oaths, or He clarifies their ruling for you, so that you may be pious, God-conscious, and set things right between people.

It is also possible that *urḍah* (عرضة) means that which is frequently exposed to something. In this sense, it would be a prohibition against swearing by Allah, the Glorified, excessively. The meaning would then be: Do not swear by Allah too much, for if you do, it will lead you to not being pious, not being God-conscious, and not reconciling between people. This is because a person who swears oaths (الحلاف) excessively does not hold the One by whom he swears in high esteem; rather, the matter of the oath becomes trivial to him due to its frequent use. Consequently, he becomes indifferent to lying and does so frequently within himself. Likewise, his status diminishes and his worth falls in the eyes of people, as they sense that he does not see himself as having a footing of truthfulness (قدم صدق) among them, believing that they will not believe what he says, nor does he honor himself by relying on his own word. This would be similar to His saying: **وَلَا تُطِعْ كُلَّ حَلَّافٍ مَّهِينٍ** — **"And do not obey any vile swearer"** (68:10). According to this interpretation, it is also more appropriate not to assume a suppressed "no" in the text; rather, the phrase **أن تبروا** would be in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition (منصوب بنزع الخافض) or as an object of purpose (مفعول له) for what the prohibition indicates, as previously mentioned.

In His saying, the Exalted: **والله سميع عليم** (And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), there is a type of threat (تهديد) applicable to all the meanings, though the first meaning is the most apparent, as is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا يؤاخذكم الله باللغو في أيمانكم** (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths...) until the end of the verse. Vain (اللغو) refers to those actions that do not entail any consequence (أثر). The consequence of a thing varies according to its different aspects and attachments. An oath has a consequence insofar as it is a word, a consequence insofar as it emphasizes speech, a consequence insofar as it is a binding contract (عقد), and a consequence insofar as its breach (حنث) and the violation of its content, and so on. However, the contrast in the verse between the lack of accountability for vain oaths and the accountability for what the hearts have earned (ما كسبته القلوب)—specifically regarding oaths—indicates that "vain oaths" refers to those that do not stem from the intention (قصد) of the swearer. This refers to oaths where the person does not intend to bind themselves to anything, such as saying "No, by Allah" or "Yes, by Allah" out of habit.

Earning (الكسب) is the acquisition of benefits through action, such as a craft, a profession, or the like. Its origin lies in the acquisition of things that fulfill a person's material needs. It was then used metaphorically for everything a person acquires through any of their actions, whether good or evil—such as earning praise, pride, and a good reputation through good character and social service; or earning a virtuous character, beneficial knowledge, and excellence through appropriate deeds; or earning blame, censure, curses, criticism, sins, and transgressions through actions that entail such consequences. This is the meaning of *kasb* (الكسب) and *iktisāb* (الاكتساب). It has been said that the difference between them is that *iktisāb* is a person's acquisition of benefit for themselves, while *kasb* is more general, whether for oneself or for another—such as a slave's earning for his master or a guardian's earning for his ward.

In any case, the one who earns (الكاسب) or acquires (المكتسب) is the human being and none other.

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of the Heart in the Quran)

This is among the evidences that what is meant by the heart (القلب) is the human being in the sense of the soul (النفس) and the spirit (الروح). While intellect (التعقل), reflection (التفكر), love (الحب), hatred (البغض), fear (الخوف), and the like can be attributed to the heart based on the belief that it is the perceiving organ in the body—as the common folk might believe, just as hearing is attributed to the ear, sight to the eye, and taste to the tongue—earning (الكسب) and acquisition (الاكتساب) are things that can absolutely only be attributed to the human being.

Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ** — **"his heart is indeed sinful"** (2:283), and His saying: **وَجَاءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ** — **"and comes with a penitent heart"** (50:33).

It appears that when man observed himself and other species of animals and reflected upon them, he saw that consciousness (الشعور) and perception (الادراك) might cease or vanish from the animal due to fainting, epilepsy, or the like, while life—indicated by the movement and beating of the heart—remains. Conversely, if the heart stops, life ceases. Thus, he concluded that the principle of life (مبدء الحياة) is the heart; that is, the spirit (الروح) which he believes exists in the animal first attaches itself to the heart, even if it then spreads from it to all the organs of life. He concluded that spiritual effects and properties—such as intuitive sensations like consciousness, will, love, hatred, hope, fear, and the like—all belong to the heart by virtue of it being the primary attachment of the spirit. This does not contradict the fact that each organ is the principle for its own specific action—such as the brain for thought, the eye for sight, the ear for hearing, and the lungs for breathing. For all of these are like instruments (الآلات) through which actions requiring an intermediary instrument are performed.

This view may be supported by scientific experiments which found that birds do not die upon the loss of the brain, though they lose perception and become unconscious of anything, remaining in that state until they die from lack of nutrition and the heart stopping its beat.

It may also be supported by the fact that natural scientific research has not succeeded to this day in identifying the source from which bodily judgments originate—that is, the throne of commands (عرش الاوامر) obeyed by the active organs in the human body. For there is no doubt that these organs, despite their inherent diversity and the separation of their functions, are gathered under one banner and led by a single commander with a true unity (وحدة حقيقية).

One should not imagine that this [attribution to the heart] arose from ignorance regarding the brain and its specific role in perception. Indeed, man has been aware of the importance of the head since the most ancient times. The evidence for this is what we see in all nations and peoples, despite their different languages, in naming the principle of judgment and command "the head" (الرأس), and the derivation of various terms from it—such as "head" (الرأس), "chief" (الرئيس), "leadership" (الرئاسة), the "head" of a thread, the "head" of a period, the "head" of a distance, the "head" of a speech, the "head" of a mountain, a "head" of cattle, and the "head" (hilt) of a sword.

This—as it appears—is the reason for their attributing perception, consciousness, and things not devoid of a trace of perception—such as love, hatred, hope, fear, intent, envy, chastity, courage, boldness, and the like—to the heart. By "heart," they mean the spirit (الروح) attached to the body or flowing through it by means of the heart. Thus, they attribute these things to it just as they attribute them to the spirit and to themselves. It is said: "I loved him," "my spirit loved him," "my soul loved him," and "my heart loved him." Eventually, this metaphorical usage became established, and "heart" was used to mean the soul (النفس) metaphorically. They even extended this to the "chest" (الصدر), making it—because it contains the heart—the locus for various types of perception and spiritual actions and attributes.

In the Quran, there is much of this nature. The Exalted said: **يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ** — **"He opens his breast to Islam"** (6:125); and He said: **أَنَّكَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرُكَ** — **"that your breast is straitened"** (18:97); and He said: **وَبَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَنَاجِرَ** — **"and the hearts reached the throats"** (33:10), which is a metaphor for the constriction of the chest. He also said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ** — **"Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts"** (5:7). It is not unlikely that these usages in His Book, the Exalted, are an indication of the reality of this view, even if it has not yet become entirely clear.

Sheikh Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna) preferred the view that perception belongs to the heart, in the sense that the brain's involvement in it is that of an instrument; thus, the heart possesses perception while the brain serves as the intermediary.

Let us return to the verse. His saying, the Exalted: **ولكن يؤاخذكم بما كسبت قلوبكم** (but He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned) is not devoid of a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي). The apparent shift from the lack of accountability for one type of oath—the vain—to another type would imply accountability for the oath itself. However, this was diverted to its consequence, which is the sin (الاثم) resulting from it upon breach (حنث). Thus, there is a rational metaphor and a shift within a shift to indicate that Allah, the Glorified, is concerned only with the heart, as He said: **وَإِن تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُم بِهِ اللَّهُ** — **"Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it"** (2:284), and He said: **وَلَٰكِن يَنَالُهُ التَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ** — **"but it is your God-consciousness that reaches Him"** (22:37).

In His saying, the Exalted: **والله غفور حليم** (And Allah is All-forgiving, All-forbearing), there is an indication of the dislike of vain oaths, for they are not something that should proceed from a believer. The Exalted has said: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — **"Certainly the faithful have attained salvation"** (23:1) until He said: **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنِ اللَّغْوِ مُعْرِضُونَ** — **"and who avoid vain talk"** (23:3).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **للذين يؤلون من نسائهم** (For those who forswear their wives...) until the end of the verse. *Īlāʾ* (الايلاء) is from *aliyyah* (الالية), meaning an oath. In the *Sharīʿah*, it became predominantly used for a husband's oath not to approach his wife out of anger or to cause harm, and this is what is intended in the verse. *Tarabbuṣ* (التربص) is waiting (الانتظار), and *fayʾ* (الفىء) is returning (الرجوع).

It appears that *īlāʾ* is made transitive with the preposition *min* (من) because it encompasses the meaning of distancing (الابتعاد) and the like, thus signifying that the oath is taken to avoid sexual intercourse. This is suggested by the limitation of the waiting period to four months, as this is the maximum period set for mandatory intercourse in the *Sharīʿah*. From this, it is known that "resolving on divorce" (العزم على الطلاق) means the resolve accompanied by its execution. This is also suggested by the concluding phrase: **فإِن الله سميع عليم** (for Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing), as hearing (السمع) relates to the divorce that has occurred, not merely the resolve for it.

In His saying, the Exalted: **فإِن الله غفور رحيم** (then Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful), there is evidence that there is no punishment for *īlāʾ* if one returns (الفيىء). As for the expiation (الكفارة), it is a legal ruling (حكم شرعي) that is not subject to forgiveness. The Exalted said: **لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمَانِكُمْ وَلَٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ الْأَيْمَانَ فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَاكِينَ** — **"Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have bound. Its expiation is feeding ten needy persons..."** (5:89).

The meaning is that for one who forswears his wife, the judge allows him a period of four months. If he returns to the marital right—which is intercourse—and expiates and has intercourse, there is no punishment upon him. If he resolves on divorce and executes it, that is the other way out. And Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī*, narrated from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ولا تجعلوا الله عرضة لايمانكم** (Do not make Allah an obstacle to your oaths...), he (peace be upon him) said: "It is a man's saying: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah'."

Also in it, from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them) regarding the verse: "It means a man who swears that he will not speak to his brother and the like, or that he will not speak to his mother."

In *al-Kāfī*, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "When you are called to reconcile between two people, do not say: 'I have an oath not to do so'."

**I say:** The first narration, as you see, interprets the verse according to one of the two meanings, while the second and third interpret it according to the other meaning. Close to these two is what is also in *Tafsīr al-ʿAyyāshī* from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them), who said: "It is a man who reconciles between two men and bears the sin that was between them..." (the tradition). It is as if the meaning is that he should not swear [to refrain], but rather reconcile and bear the sin, and Allah will forgive him; thus, he would be an instance of one who acts upon the verse.

In *al-Kāfī*, from Masʿadah, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لا يؤاخذكم الله باللغو في ايمانكم** (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths...), he said: "Vain (اللغو) is a man's saying: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah' without binding himself to anything."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in *al-Kāfī* from him (peace be upon him) through another chain, and in *al-Majmaʿ* from him and from Imam al-Bāqir (peace be upon him).

Also in *al-Kāfī*, from Imam al-Bāqir and Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon them), they said: "When a man forswears (آلى) approaching his wife, she has no say or right during the four months, and there is no sin upon him for refraining from her during the four months. If the four months pass before he touches her, then if she remains silent and is content, he is in a state of ease and latitude. But if she takes her case [to the judge], he is told: 'Either you return (تفيء) and touch her, or you divorce.' And the resolve on divorce (عزم الطلاق) is that he leaves her alone; then when she menstruates and becomes pure, he divorces her. He has more right to take her back as long as three menstrual cycles (قروء) have not passed. This is the *īlāʾ* which Allah sent down in His Book and which the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) established as a Sunnah."

Also in it, from Imam al-Ṣādiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "And *īlāʾ* is that he says: 'By Allah, I will not have intercourse with you for such and such [a time],' or he says: 'By Allah, I will surely vex you,' and then he vexes her..." (the tradition).

**I say:** Regarding the specific details of *īlāʾ* and some matters related to it, there is disagreement between the Sunnis and the Shi'a, and the discussion is a juristic one mentioned in the books of jurisprudence (الفقه).
